data.title: QuantumScape: No Longer An Option, Now A Blueprint
data.description:
QuantumScape's (QS) price action suggests a good zone for an opportunistic Buy on the technical progress made. For a pre-revenue stock, measuring narrative progress is a difficult task. I have tried to formalize my arguments through a measurable framework for progress in terms of how many commercialization milestones have been de-risked over time. While the absolute framework could be up for debate, it provides useful insight into the magnitude of progress made.

I anchor my observations around Q2 2023, when the stock was primarily valued as a lab optionality. At the time, manufacturing planning was largely conceptual, and automotive partnerships were still exploratory. Markets were at a stage when they were exiting the rate-shocked lows of 2022-23, not really a market that was still fully rewarding growth. The 2025 lows and subsequent highs were distorted by market conditions, first going through a panic crash in April 2025, followed by a narrative supportive phase where several pre-revenue narrative bets rallied sharply.

As the momentum has slowed now, a comparison with Q2 2023 as an anchor should provide a cleaner comparison of progress made and how much the valuations have evolved since. I feel that this 2-3 year gap also helps filter out short-term market noise and narrative swings, providing a more realistic view of structural progress than comparisons driven by recent sentiment alone.

In terms of share prices alone, it is only up by over 20% since Q2 2023. Even the market cap is up by 67% only. That seems reasonable compared to progress made during this time. And that assessment includes any potential impact of time-to-market expectations that may have reset during roughly the past couple of years.

Progress Made Over Time

The summary of progress made between Q2 2023 and today is that what was once a question around arriving at a real product has now transformed into a real battery company answering manufacturing at scale questions. In 2023, QuantumScape was in the process of installing Raptor equipment and talking about Cobra as a future higher throughput process. They were running the early QS-0 pre-production line. This was a stage where the focus was on being able to build these batteries outside the lab. Today, QuantumScape has clearly moved into an early factory workflow stage. They have now shipped Cobra-based QSE-5 B1 samples, installed key equipment on the more automated Eagle pilot line, and even partnered with Murata and Corning to scale production of the ceramic separator. QS's earlier narrative implied they would have to solve large scale manufacturing mostly on their own. They are clearly leaning on industrial manufacturing experts, lowering execution risks. We are still not talking about yield rates and volumes, but QuantumScape has clearly moved out of the lab and is addressing manufacturing at scale questions.

We also are starting to see battery performance data being collected. QS batteries were used in a Ducati V21L electric race motorcycle demonstration, and greater field testing is being discussed. Again, we do not have durability or cycle life data, but we are seeing real-world validation. That's progress from where we were in 2023, when unit testing in the lab was in play.

The automotive customer-level engagement has also materially progressed. The Volkswagen Group is tied to the Ducati demo. An OEM is in a formal joint development agreement, and a large automaker is mentioned as being in active engagement. This is progress to the formal evaluation stage, although not yet production contracts or agreements.

In Q3 2025, QS reported $12.8m in customer billings. Though not a proof of commercialization yet, even minor payments for developmental work is progress from no customer revenues or billings in 2023. The runway has also received a boost with $1b liquidity reported in Q3 2025, and runway estimates extended till around 2030.

De-Risking Framework

My de-risking framework attempts to quantify progress to check whether the ~22% change in share prices over the past couple of years is too much. Not all milestones matter equally for valuations, so weights have been assigned to each factor that should be tracked for a pre-revenue industrial tech company. The exact weights I assign to each risk or stage of progress may be debatable, but I have tried to keep things conservative and hope to quantify the progress, even in a broad range.

For QS, two broad questions are what defines the risks to commercialization - whether the tech can be manufactured at automotive scale, and how long and how uncertain the path to commercialization is. Economics beyond commercialization is a different stage for investments altogether.

I have kept the two most relevant broad questions today in mind and assigned a high weightage to manufacturing uncertainties - because the ability to produce batteries that work at scale with consistent quality and yield ultimately decides whether the technology can become a viable business proposition. Automaker validation is another critical area because eventually confirmation from end customers shows promise and confidence in the tech offering. Battery performance remains important, but I have assigned it a slightly lower (still material) weight. The lower weight acknowledges some of the lab feasibility tests and early validation that have been done till now, and the experimental deployment, like in the Ducati demo, shows pending validation is important but incremental. I have also assigned a reasonably high weight to time to market, because valuations are definitely affected by a longer time to market and commercialization uncertainties. Financial runway is weighed slightly less because it impacts risk of dilution and execution delays, but is not directly a determinant of commercialization success. The final factors in my model - commercial traction, partnerships, and execution track record - build credibility and are assigned relatively minor weights.

The framework outlines what I have noted in the Progress Section already, but provides a quantitative lens to view the indicative magnitude of progress. My framework suggests QS is still far from commercialization, but has decisively moved ahead from an early feasibility stage too. The weighted average score has improved from ~78% risks open to ~56% risks open at this stage - using my conservative estimates. A meaningful portion of core risks has been reduced - especially around manufacturing direction, OEM engagement, ecosystem support, and financial survival.

I have kept some factors still similarly positioned (conservative). Time to market is one factor where, although I feel a late decade commercialization expectation is better understood now, removing uncertainties compared to where we were in 2023. However, markets may have assumed earlier commercialization in 2023, and hence my minimal change in progress numbers allows for the benefit of the doubt that should drag valuations. It is also true that high volume manufacturing proof is still pending and contracts are yet to be signed - something investors should now focus on, with several risks materially reduced.

The bridge to ~67% market cap expansion and ~22% share price increase while the risks have reduced from ~78% to ~56% is still subjective. I feel for the magnitude of progress made versus where the share is trading at now, favorably tilts entries toward opportunistic buys, especially after corrections from the 2025 tops. QS is no longer just an option in my view, which it was in 2023. Now it has a tangible blueprint and remaining risks that can be tracked far easier. That should count for more than a mere double-digit growth in share prices/valuations.

What to Watch

The remaining risks to the thesis have been highlighted throughout the thesis, but I collate them here again for discussion. While QS has demonstrated pilot level capabilities and is close to a factory stage, yield rates and cost metrics are still unknown at high volume throughput. The time to contract wins and qualification cycle uncertainties are also open. Meaningful revenues are several years out. Any delay in B-sample or C-sample validation, or factory readiness, would push revenue further out and pressure valuation, as well as raise financial or dilution risks. Detailed performance data is pending. Economics compared to lithium-ion alternatives are also a big deal - but I ignore that in my framework as of today because that is a topic that becomes more relevant closer to commercialization.

These remaining risks (a reduced list from 2023) are what will be watched in the upcoming quarters, including Q4 2025. Any manufacturing data, like throughput or yield indications, will be important to track. Production readiness on the Eagle line would materially strengthen the thesis. If Q4 misses these operational details, it could still be overlooked, but a lack of such details over the next 3-4 quarters should raise concerns.

Updates on B1 sample performance, even subjective, would be welcome. Partnerships with automotive companies, OEMs, and manufacturers should remain positive throughout this commercial readiness phase. It is also important to meet the capex guidance for the full year to be between $30-40m. And EBITDA losses are to remain between $245m-$260m. I will also await any guidance issues for the next financial year.

Overall, I don't expect Q4 to be a make or break quarter. Fresh Buys today will have to watch for progress indicators over the next 3-4 quarters and be ready to remain patient through volatility. Markets in the near term may remain cautious about narrative-based pre-revenue stocks, so investors should look at a window of at least a year from now and be comfortable riding deep losses through this period. My analysis focused on the progress made over a little over 2 years deliberately, because that kind of time lapse is long enough to cut out interim noise. Otherwise, QS has seen triple digit returns and 40-50% corrections quite a few times in the interim. The end-to-end ~22% uptick in share prices lives through this volatility and ends up looking more aligned to the magnitude of progress made.

data.title: Greystone Capital Q4 2025 Portfolio Analysis
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Greystone Capital Q4 2025 Letter To Clients.

Looking back, there were three primary contributors to underperformance during 2025, driven mostly by portfolio-specific outcomes rather than broad market conditions. First, there were instances of poor stock selection where I was wrong about business trajectory, earnings power or management quality (Sylogist (SYZ.TO), Franklin Covey (FC)). Second, several holdings that performed strongly in 2024 (Limbach (LMB), Natural Resource Partners, Medical Facilities Corp., Innovative Foods Holdings (IVFH)) experienced valuation compression or flat performance despite strong operational progress. Third, we have largely avoided AI themed investments, which proved to be a meaningful headwind versus the indices. Negative contributors were offset by strong performance from APi Group, KITS Eyecare, Bel Fuse (BELFA) and Leon’s Furniture (LNF.TO).

In hindsight, I could have prevented some performance detraction by reducing our exposure to certain investments that had appreciated strongly going into and during the year, but in more than a decade of managing capital, my willingness to stand by our investments so long as the fundamental story hasn’t changed, has indeed been the right approach. That’s because this strategy has minimized our investment mistakes and has allowed for the efficient compounding of your capital.

Importantly, underperformance at the portfolio level did not equate to poor business performance. Several holdings delivered record operating results while their share prices failed to reflect that progress. Natural Resource Partners finished the year roughly flat despite a very strong year of free cash flow generation and debt reduction, bringing us one step closer to increased distributions. Medical Facilities declined modestly despite substantial share repurchases, asset monetization, and continued cash generation, and Limbach finished the year down significantly despite record results and continued execution in its higher-quality ODR segment.

As a result of both mistakes and opportunity cost, throughout the year, we exited the positions where our original thesis no longer held and reallocated capital toward businesses with higher quality, more visible earnings power, and more attractive forward return profiles. While this creates the appearance of selling after periods of weak performance, these decisions were driven by relative risk-reward and long-term expected returns, not short-term price action. The cumulative effect of these decisions is a portfolio built around fewer variables and clearer drivers of long-term value.

To that end, attractive returns from here do not require heroic assumptions or perfect execution, only that the world continues to function as it already does. While none of our businesses are flawless, each is deeply embedded in critical systems, supported by recurring demand, aligned incentives, and a demonstrated ability to grow per-share value over time. Our approach is to allow these businesses to compound through short-term noise and periodic volatility, with the underlying bet being a straightforward one; global coal usage will remain durable over the next 3–5 years, access to cost-effective eyecare will continue to expand, commercial buildings will require ongoing fire and life-safety maintenance, and hospitals will continue performing surgical procedures.

Although our concentrated portfolio will inevitably experience bouts of volatility, I feel very good about the odds of delivering a satisfactory long-term outcome from today’s starting point. My family and I remain large investors in Greystone, firmly aligning our interests.

Portfolio Commentary

At year end, our top five holdings, representing nearly 75% of our capital, consist of Natural Resource Partners, KITS Eyecare, our undisclosed position, APi Group and Medical Facilities Corp.

At the risk of sounding like a broken record, the current opportunity set remains unusually attractive. In addition to the undisclosed position discussed below, we initiated a new core holding that I believe can deliver compelling long-term returns during the next few years, which I discuss in the Appendix at the end of this letter.

We also established two smaller positions in very different businesses, each characterized by durable competitive advantages, strong free-cash-flow generation and valuations that remain reasonable relative to their long-term prospects. I look forward to disclosing more over time.

Top Five Positions

Undisclosed Position

Apologies for the opaqueness here, but we are still actively buying this stock, and I have not disclosed anything publicly yet. In addition, Greystone Capital is currently raising capital for a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) to invest in this business.

This market-leading, asset-light service business benefits from meaningful geographic and regulatory barriers that support stable, recession-resistant cash flows and allow reinvestment at mid-teens to low-twenties returns. Despite predictable free cash flow, limited competition, and a balance sheet that enables both aggressive share repurchases and disciplined reinvestment, the company continues to trade as a lower-quality, cyclical service provider. I believe this mispricing reflects an outdated view of the business rather than its current reality. Even assuming no multiple expansion, ongoing free cash flow generation, reinvestment, and capital returns support attractive forward returns from today’s valuation.

What makes this opportunity especially attractive is the combination of underappreciated compounding characteristics and a management team that understands capital allocation at a very high level. The purpose of the SPV is to build a concentrated position in this business at a meaningful discount to intrinsic value. Please feel free to reach out for more information.

Natural Resource Partners (NRP)

Natural Resource Partners, our coal royalty business, delivered another strong year in 2025, continuing its evolution into a debt-free, capital-return-focused royalty and infrastructure business. Due to the nature of the royalty business model that avoids ongoing capital expenditures and high operating costs, cash flows remained robust across the mineral rights segment, in what has been a tough environment for both met and thermal coal prices. During 2025, global coal consumption of 8.8 billion tons once again reached record highs, and given the severe supply/demand imbalance globally, I’d imagine pricing conditions begin to change favorably as we approach the end of the decade.

In my view, the most underappreciated attribute of NRP’s business is lack of price risk being taken here. The royalty business model provides exceptional durability, having been stress-tested through various pricing environments and macro environments. Most importantly, the balance sheet continues to strengthen, and leverage metrics now sit comfortably within long-term targets, giving management flexibility to increase shareholder distributions within a few quarters. With a 20-year track record of positive free cash flow and plenty of reserves in the ground, I like our odds to collect distributions from NRP well into the foreseeable future.

As a reminder, we have no way of forecasting the price of coal, and we are not underwriting heroic growth assumptions. NRP is positioned to generate significant free cash flow during the next several years, much of which should be returned to us. We also get to capture the upside of any exuberance in coal prices along with the eventual recovery of soda ash. Shares trade at a substantial discount to my intrinsic value estimate of $200-250/share, making NRP one of the most compelling asymmetries in the portfolio.

KITS Eyecare (KITS.TO)

KITS Eyecare, our e-commerce eyecare business, continued to execute exceptionally well through 2025, further validating the strength of its vertically integrated, direct-to-consumer eyecare model. KITS is exactly the type of business we want to own through volatility: founder-led, operationally disciplined and focused on long-term value creation rather than near-term optics. While valuation will occasionally invite debate, our focus remains on the durability of the cash flows moving forward. KITS is an innovative, customer-focused business that has built a better, and more cost-effective model for purchasing glasses and contacts. Despite low brand awareness, especially in the US, KITS has found product/market/fit, and 2026 is on pace to be another record year of revenue and EBITDA.

Despite being just eight years old as a business, KITS is one of the fastest organically growing businesses in Canada, and has achieved the holy grail of e-commerce, selling into a huge TAM combined with strong unit economics in the form of an incredibly favorable CAC/LTV ratio. KITS refusal to participate in expensive, highly competitive marketing channels has allowed them to spread the word organically and in select markets across Canada and the US, clearing the way for profitable growth even as revenue accelerates 25-30% per year. Customer focus and cost discipline are woven into the culture of the business, enabling KITS to stand out from both service and price perspectives. High quality prescription glasses with lens upgrades retail on KITS.com for less than $60. Similar products at competing brands would cost between $150 – $300.

The structural advantages of KITS business model became increasingly evident in 2025. In-house manufacturing, lens edging, and fulfillment enable tighter quality control and faster inventory turns, while proprietary customer data improves marketing efficiency and lifetime value. The result is a model with high incremental margins, low working capital intensity and strong cash generation. KITS remains largely insulated from technological disruption and macro volatility, as vision correction is non-discretionary and repeat-driven. Management continues to reinvest prudently in automation, logistics, and new styles, extending the company’s competitive moat while preserving attractive unit economics. As a result, KITS continues to take share from both traditional optometry and less integrated online competitors, which I expect to continue well into the future.

At current prices, KITS trades at a premium to traditional retailers, but I believe this understates the durability and long-term potential of the model. The company is still early in its margin and cash flow inflection, and I expect earnings growth to materially outpace revenue growth over the next several years as fixed costs are leveraged, and as high margin glasses revenue becomes a larger part of total revenue. If management continues executing as it has, KITS has the potential to compound free cash flow at very high rates over a multi-year horizon. Based on my intrinsic value estimate of approximately CAD $35-$45/share, I still see a wide disconnect between price and value.

APi Group (APG)

APi Group continues to execute as a steady, high-quality business operating in mission-critical fire and life-safety services. 2025 was another strong year, characterized by mid-single-digit organic growth, disciplined pricing, expanding margins, and robust cash generation. End-market demand remains healthy, and APG continues to shift the revenue mix toward recurring inspection, monitoring, and service work, which carries higher margins, lower working capital intensity, and strong customer stickiness. APG remains one of the most economically resilient models we own with recurring revenue, critical infrastructure exposure, and zero technological displacement risk.

The balance sheet has returned to a position of strength following the Chubb (CB) acquisition and subsequent deleveraging, once again enabling the company to pursue disciplined, bolt-on M&A while continuing to grow through organic initiatives. This, along with cash generation, a long runway to reinvest, and favorable working capital dynamics makes APG recession resistant and worthy of a high multiple.

As a result, the market has finally given APG some credit for business strength and quality. Today, APG trades at 21x free cash flow and 18.5x EBITDA. While this doesn’t appear headline cheap for a single digit organic grower, I view forward returns as primarily earnings-driven rather than multiple-driven, which is exactly the type of profile I prefer. If APG achieves their three-year targets of $10B in revenues, $1.6 billion in EBITDA and 80% FCF conversion by 2028, shares should be worth $65 at current multiples for roughly 60% upside. That equates to an 18% annual return profile with limited risk, an underappreciated setup in today’s market.

Medical Facilities Corp. (DR.TO)

We’ve owned Medical Facilities Corporation for a few years and have done very well on the back of a cash generative portfolio of hospitals and a management team that is manically focused on repurchasing shares. It’s very rare to see a management team both recognize the best use of excess capital and then stick to a plan to either reinvest it or return it to shareholders. This is one such group, and as a result I believe there remains further upside.

Medical Facilities’ business model remains fundamentally attractive, consisting of ownership interests in specialized surgical hospitals with strong local market positions, high physician alignment, and favorable long-term demographic tailwinds. These assets generate durable cash flows with limited technological displacement risk and modest ongoing capital requirements. Management has remained focused on optimizing existing assets rather than pursuing dilutive growth, and then harvesting cash flow for capital returns, a strategy that maximizes per-share value over time.

During 2025, improving surgical volumes, stable physician engagement, and disciplined cost management, combined with normalization of elective procedure demand and improved labor dynamics supported margin stabilization and strong cash flow generation. As mentioned, MFC continues to allocate capital with an owner’s mindset, buying back over 20% of shares outstanding through a tender offer and open market purchases. During the past four years, MFC has reduced shares outstanding by nearly 40%.

Despite these positives, at $11.50/share, MFC trades well below my estimate of intrinsic value between $18-20/share. As cash flow generation continues and more shares are repurchased, I believe shares will continue to climb higher, with optionality for further asset sales within the portfolio. As a reminder, MFC sold one of their hospitals, Black Hills Surgical, in November of 2024, using the proceeds to repurchase $65M in stock through a tender offer. Another deal of that nature would benefit us tremendously, but even without one, we will continue to own a cheap, cash-generative asset with a management team repurchasing plenty of stock.

Disclaimer: Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Investing involves risks which clients should be prepared to bear, including but not limited to partial or complete loss of principal originally invested.Investing in small and microcap companies can result in additional volatility and higher risk due to comparatively low market capitalization, more sensitivity to economic and market conditions, and more limited managerial and financial resources. In addition, small companies typically trade in lower volume, making them more difficult to purchase or sell at the desired time and price or in the desired amount. Please refer to Form ADV Part 2 brochure for more information about Greystone Capital Management and its personnel.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CPAI: Robust Performance Merits Shortlisting, But Beware Of The Risks
data.description:
After reviewing the performance track record, portfolio composition, as well as such aspects as liquidity and expenses, I believe the Counterpoint Quantitative Equity ETF (CPAI), an actively managed exchange-traded fund leveraging AI-driven models in its investment process, is an excellent candidate for an ETF investor's short list of vehicles worth considering.

Nevertheless, while I am impressed by its returns, there are a few issues that persuaded me to stop short of a Buy rating for now, so I initiate coverage with a Hold rating.

First, CPAI's risk-adjusted returns look quite soft owing to high volatility. In this regard, I believe it is not suitable for investors seeking not just to maximize returns but also to, ideally, keep volatility around market-like levels.

Second, despite a quite healthy, almost linear growth trend, its AUM is still relatively small at just $173.14 million (as of January 27). The direct consequence here is that liquidity overall is far from perfect, with its 30-day median bid/ask spread of 0.2042% being an issue.

Third, its turnover of 324% is clearly elevated.

Fourth, the expense ratio of 75 bps is rather high. Of course, given the stock selection mastery, the high fee looks justified. Yet it nonetheless a factor that eats into the total returns.

And finally, incepted in November 2023, the ETF was not tested by a bear market.

I will address these aspects with all necessary attention below in the note.

CPAI Strategy And Portfolio

As described on its website, incepted on November 28, 2023, CPAI

...invests in individual stocks that have exposure to multiple factors and are among the highest-ranking stocks to hold as determined by quantitative models and artificial intelligence to seek outperformance relative to the market.

As explained in the summary prospectus, machine learning is at the core of the process:

The quantitative models use machine learning technology to rank stocks of companies of U.S. domiciled companies and ADRs of any market capitalization.

Quite a few variables are incorporated:

The Fund’s models use more than 30 variables as inputs. These variables revolve around themes of value, long-term reversal, stock price momentum, company profitability, investor sentiment toward the company, and stock price stability.

How frequently are the adjustments made to the CPAI portfolio? I will answer with a quote:

At least every quarter, the Adviser rebalances the portfolio based on the stocks’ current rankings.

And these adjustments are anything but cosmetic: the 324% turnover speaks for itself.

CPAI is benchmarked against the S&P 1000® Index. On a side note, this is quite an exotic benchmark selection, which I rarely see in ETFs (if at all). But still, given CPAI's "focus on small and mid-cap companies," the choice makes perfect sense, as the index is, essentially, the sum of the S&P SmallCap 600 Index and the S&P MidCap 400 Index, the barometers of the U.S. mid-cap and small-cap equity universes.

CPAI's Current Positioning Is Contrarian

What is CPAI's current factor story, and how does it compare to that of the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) (which I nearly always use as a market proxy)? In a word, from the data I see, I can confidently say that it is unambiguously contrarian, as the fund puts emphasis on the value factor, favoring sectors that are underrepresented in the S&P 500, as well as low volatility. It might sound odd, but its exposure to momentum is also fairly large (yes, low-beta stocks might sometimes demonstrate momentum characteristics). Meanwhile, CPAI almost ignores quality, and growth characteristics are nice but not outstanding. I will be giving much more color on all these aspects shortly. But first, let us look at the top ten holdings.

As of January 23, there were 50 common stocks in the CPAI portfolio, with the main holding being NioCorp Developments Ltd., with a 2.57% weight.

Company Ticker Weight, as of January 23 Sector Market Cap, $ Million, as of January 27
NioCorp Developments Ltd. (NB) 2.57% Materials 830.06
Eos Energy Enterprises, Inc. (EOSE) 2.56% Industrials 5,247.15
MP Materials Corp. (MP) 2.48% Materials 11,243.50
Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) 2.44% Information Technology 437,924.40
GPGI, Inc. (GPGI) 2.33% Information Technology 7,189.08
Solid Power, Inc. (SLDP) 2.32% Consumer Discretionary 972.31
AMN Healthcare Services, Inc. (AMN) 2.32% Health Care 784.03
Herbalife Ltd. (HLF) 2.30% Consumer Staples 1,740.79
Elbit Systems Ltd. (ESLT) 2.23% Industrials 33,170.47
AngloGold Ashanti plc (AU) 2.22% Materials 54,573.28

Created by the author using data from the ETF and Seeking Alpha

How unique are exposures offered? I would say that they are rather unique and unorthodox. More specifically, my analysis shows that out of the key ten stocks presented above, only three names have qualified for either IVV or the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) and the iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH), which track the indices that together form CPAI's benchmark:

  • MP Materials Corp. can be found in the IJH portfolio.
  • AMN is the S&P 600 constituent.
  • Micron Technology is an IT bellwether with a place in the S&P 500.

Moreover, just 18% of CPAI's net assets are allocated to S&P 500 companies, as per my calculations. At the same time, eight names (16.06% weight) are the S&P 600 constituents, and five (10.39%) have qualified for the IJH ETF.

Importantly, CPAI has only modest exposure to the trillion-dollar league via Alphabet Inc., represented by Class A stock (GOOGL) with 1.87% weight, while 63.6% of its net assets are allocated to SMID names (less than $10 billion in market cap), so its weighted average market cap stands at $99.85 billion, as per my calculations.

As to sectors, at this point, CPAI is quite heavy in health care (in contrast to IJR, IJH, and IVV), while it completely ignores real estate and utilities.

So, returning to my point that CPAI offers contrarian exposures, the weighted average metrics I compiled in the table below illustrate this issue quite clearly.

Metric CPAI IVV
Adjusted weighted average P/S 7.12 8.69
Adjusted weighted average EV/EBITDA 11.12 21.54
Quant Valuation B- or higher 34.39% 9.77%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs. Based on the financial data as of January 27.

An important remark is that I decided to remove the earnings yield from the analysis this time, as the metric makes little sense: 30 out of 50 companies in the CPAI portfolio (a massive 60.4% weight) are loss-making, so even a few adjustments will not help much.

As to the quality factor, just 48.4% of CPAI's holdings are currently boasting a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher, while IVV has over a 94% allocation to such names. Moreover, even IJR has a larger exposure to such companies (approximately 50.6%).

Speaking of low volatility and momentum, the CPAI portfolio is offering significant exposure to both, sporting lower weighted average beta coefficients than IVV and a notably larger allocation to stocks with a Quant Momentum grade of B- or higher.

Metric CPAI IVV
24M Beta 0.84 1.11
60M Beta 0.95 1.13
Quant Momentum B- or higher 73.48% 60.34%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Finally, the situation with CPAI's growth factor exposure is quite nuanced, as its weighted average forward growth metrics are meaningfully lower than IVV's, yet its allocation to stocks with a Quant Growth grade of B- or stronger is larger.

Metric CPAI IVV
Revenue Fwd 12.66% 15.52%
EPS Fwd 13.22% 22.51%
EBITDA Fwd 10.62% 19.95%
Quant Growth B- or higher 53.55% 44.30%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

CPAI Performance: Impressive, But Imperfections Are Not To Be Overlooked

First and foremost, since its inception in November 2023, CPAI has substantially outperformed the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF and the iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF, which track the S&P 600 and the S&P 400, respectively.

But what is much more impressive is that it has trounced the market proxied with IVV, as well as the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) and the Amplify AI Powered Equity ETF (AIEQ), which also uses artificial intelligence in its stock selection process.

And what is notable is how robust its start to 2026 has been, in contrast to some softness IVV and QQQ have been experiencing.

However, delving deeper, we see a few issues.

First, over December 2023–December 2025, CPAI's risk-adjusted returns (measured using the Sharpe and Sortino ratios) meaningfully exceeded those of IJR and IJH, yet they were well lower than those of IVV and QQQ.

Second, the downside capture ratio of 135.54% is quite disappointing even despite the 120.71% upside capture.

Third, CPAI's maximum drawdown (delivered in April 2025) was not that deep compared to the S&P 400 and S&P 600 ETFs, yet IVV's and QQQ's results were better.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, CPAI's performance since inception, secured by the machine learning models and quarterly (at least) adjustments to the portfolio, seemingly leaves little room for skepticism.

But while its robustness is, of course, beyond any doubt, the fixation on total or price returns in the ETF research and selection process is one of the issues that I have always advised against. So, other factors that do matter and should be reviewed carefully, like risk-adjusted performance, expenses, and liquidity, are not supportive of a bullish thesis.

Besides, CPAI has been capable of successfully navigating the bull market driven by the low-inflation and AI narratives, but we do not know how its performance would have looked in much less buoyant conditions, like those in 2022.

All in all, while acknowledging its advantages, I am stopping short of the Buy rating.

data.title: LVMH: Resilient In 2025, But Still Not A Compelling Entry Point
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered LVMH Moët Hennessy - Louis Vuitton (LVMHF) (LVMUY), I downgraded them to a Hold after the risk-reward deteriorated following the stock rising ~25% despite macro pressure rising and potentially delaying the company’s recovery, while their previous results certainly did not help.

Despite an overall solid Q4 2025 report that continued to prove their resilience and strong execution, the stock continues to trade at overall elevated valuations that leave little room for error in an uncertain market, which is why I’ll continue rating LVMH a Hold, for now.

Internal Developments

Q4 and 2025 were not bad overall, delivering what the company called “solid performance in a disrupted global economic and geopolitical environment,” while the organic revenue was nearly flat, with modest growth in watches & jewelry and selective retailing offsetting the decline in wines & spirits and fashion.

As we can see, the weakness in Asia and especially Japan improved in Q4, although the overall results were still marked by the significant drops seen earlier in 2025, while the US and Europe were relatively flat in terms of organic revenue, but the company remains confident in their ability to continue improving in 2026 despite the uncertain environment.

Regarding the free cash flow, we can see a strong €14.3 billion, a solid improvement compared to €13.39 billion in 2024 and €10.92 billion in 2023, highlighting an overall strong performance and even resilience despite the weak environment, with the company rapidly cutting their CAPEX significantly in response to the environment.

Financially, based on LVMH’s latest report, we see an overall strong position, with the current assets covering their current liabilities and their long-term borrowings combined, with their ~€8.8 billion in cash and cash equivalents providing significant financial flexibility that should help them navigate the uncertain environment and even take advantage of it.

Macro Developments

In recent months, more and more studies highlighted the rising stress and financial instability of the US consumer, and I believe it’s important to note that the company is far from immune to this kind of weakness, as we can see. This comes at a time when the US consumer (similar to the rest) has been affected by the pandemic, followed by the high-rate environment pushing discretionary budgets lower, while the cost of living continued to rise and job insecurity continued to cause pressure.

Meanwhile, the situation in Greenland certainly took its toll on the stock, as the company could’ve been caught in the middle of a renewed “trade war,” and even though we saw a Trump reaching a framework, the fear around the situation still persists.

A few days after that, we saw a new 100% tariff threat on Canada due to news of their agreement with China, which can just add even more risk for the market to take into account. As a note, LVMH faces even greater risk from this in case it “spreads” more towards reignited China trade tensions, potentially pressuring an already weak environment.

Ultimately, the current socio-politico-economic situation in the US can hurt the market’s trust and cause a “higher-for-longer” rate environment, with worries about the Fed’s independence, uncertainty around rate cuts, the weakening dollar, and even the rising national debt eventually being reflected in the treasuries, which impact the whole economy.

Regarding the latter, the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget (CRFB) recently warned that the rising national debt could soon grow faster than the US economy, which they say could trigger “six distinct types of fiscal crises,” and I believe it’s no surprise that gold, silver, and other metals continue reaching higher levels day after day.

However, although it can be bad for their US operations, this has potential to help foreign assets like LVMH, as money could move away from US assets into foreign ones (with LVMH being one of the largest companies globally).

My fears around that were recently confirmed, with a couple of big North European investors reportedly considering a shift away from the US given the rising risks, while two Nordic pension funds (Sweden's Alecta and Denmark's AkademikerPension) sold or being in the process of selling their US treasuries, and I believe that if this continues, there is certainly a good possibility that we’re looking at the beginning of a wider diversification effort that can punish US equities at least in the near future.

Still, LVMH remains in an overall premium spot thanks to their valuation and relatively resilient operations, with potential for them to keep expanding while the broader industry feels more pressure, with potential to keep investing and developing brands like Armani - which I mentioned in the previous analysis, as LVMH was among the preferred buyers of a minority stake in the company, about which LVMH only commented that they're “very honored” back during the Q3 Earnings Call.

However, that doesn’t mean this is a buy no matter what, and we’ve seen other analysts, including a team at Morgan Stanley downgrading the company lately (to Equal-weight) as the risk-reward shifted in an unfavorable direction following the recent hike in price combined with tariff headwinds.

Morgan Stanley warned that the company will only partially be able to offset the potential effects of FX and tariffs through price increases to avoid pricing out the middle-income consumer, while expecting their wine and spirits division to fall to a profit of €988 million (i.e. 18.1% margin) in 2026, the lowest level since 2010 due to various other macro challenges.

Risks Reiterated

Although LVMH is definitely able and has proven their ability to capitalize on their premium valuation to expand and should be more resilient thanks to middle- and high-income consumer exposure, that doesn’t mean they are safe from an economic downturn, and there’s certainly room for things to get worse.

Meanwhile, their main markets can face tariff pressure, and even if the company aimed at improving their local production capacity, that will still take time, while the market can certainly "tax" their valuation if it expects these headwinds to continue, which might very likely be the case at least in the near future.

Valuation

Starting with LVMH’s free cash flow of €14.3 billion, I’m going to convert it into the equivalent of ~$17.13 billion for this DCF to equity valuation. I’ll assume a CAGR of 8% over the first 5 years (not far even from the market’s revenue expectations) to take into account the company’s ability to use their strong position to develop while the environment could also improve (especially in Asia) and debt costs can fall, followed by a 5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years as they continue to capitalize on their strong position and expand, with a 3% terminal growth rate and a 10% discount rate to account for a decent margin of safety, as well as 497.98 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 2025 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $331.44 billion, for an intrinsic value of $665.56 per LVMHF share compared to $685.05 at the moment of writing this (or $133.11 vs. the current $138.38 for LVMUY), which still doesn’t seem to offer an opportunity anymore.

Conclusions

Despite the strong performance in 2025, I maintain my Hold rating for LVMH, as the company is already trading around what I’d consider a fair value given their strong potential and momentum, leaving little room for error.

I like the company, and I certainly see the potential and appeal in owning it, but that doesn’t justify buying at any price, as the reward ultimately has to justify the risks it comes with.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fossil Group: Fighting A Declining Market
data.description:
Fossil Group, Inc. (FOSL) is at an interesting point in time. A secular decline in the fashion watch market and Fossil’s operational missteps have made the company’s earnings performance weak, leading to the stock's sharp long-term decline. The stock has more recently caught the market’s attention, though – Fossil’s shares have doubled during the past year as the company's turnaround plan has shown progress. Insider purchases have reaffirmed the market's faith in a turnaround. Regardless, I remain skeptical of Fossil’s ability to successfully and sustainably turn around earnings.

A Very Brief Introduction of Fossil

Fossil designs, sources, markets, and distributes a range of fashion accessories, primarily watches, through a number of brands. The company owns the Fossil watch brand among other names, but also sells a number of licensed fashion brands, including Michael Kors, Empirio Armani, and others. The brands are primarily priced in an affordable price range.

Setting the Scene: Fossil’s Sales Are on a Steep Decline

The long-term financial performance of Fossil hasn’t been pretty. Sales have been on a near-constant decline from 2015 forward. Fossil’s sales currently stand at $1066 million, down at a sharp -10.7% CAGR from $3229 million in 2015. Fossil guides a mid-teens revenue decline for 2025, and that adjusted operating income will be “breakeven to slightly positive”. Weakening sales continue to cause significant margin pressure, as Fossil is struggling to maintain the cost base at a corresponding level to generate healthy earnings.

Fossil’s concerning trend coincides with the launch of the Apple Watch in 2015 – the market for mechanical fashion watches has been dwindling since. Consumers are shifting from fashion watches to smartwatches for their better functionality. Fossil previously tried to enter the smartwatch category in 2016, but wasn’t ultimately able to compete with Apple (AAPL), Samsung (SSNLF), and others. The rise of smartwatches continues to cause pressure on sales. The rise of dropshipped and white-label watch microbrands has caused increasing competitive pressure in an already weak market.

Fossil hasn’t been the only struggling watch company. Movado Group (MOV) has also had struggles with maintaining earnings. Switzerland’s watch industry federation data has shown a continued decline in the value of watch exports, shown especially sharply in the <CHF 200 (<$255) price range. In November 2025, the lowest price range’s exports declined by -19.9%, continuing a longer trend. The price range’s exports declined by -5.9% in 2024. I previously discussed the trend in an article on Switzerland’s largest watchmaker by unit volume, Swatch Group (SWGAY).

Yet, Fossil’s decline has clearly been the sharpest, questioning Fossil’s brands’ value and the company’s operational strategy.

Making the situation more concerning, Fossil has a significant amount of debt. The company has $176 million in interest-bearing debts, pressuring earnings through interest. Luckily, Fossil managed to refinance its senior notes to 2029 maturity through restructuring. Fossil maintains good liquidity through a $150 million revolving credit facility; debt likely won’t cause immediate liquidity issues, but makes Fossil’s earnings outlook even more volatile to shareholders.

A Turnaround Plan Is in Motion, But the Outlook Is Volatile

The concerning performance led Fossil to unveil a significant multi-year turnaround plan in early 2025. The current plan includes three core pillars – refocusing the core, right-sizing the cost structure, and strengthening the balance sheet. In essence, Fossil is now refocusing the business towards its most profitable areas. Fossil previously branched into too many markets and brands, which the current market environment doesn’t support.

A renewed Fossil brand is one key turnaround driver – a new brand website, new products, heritage brand storytelling, and new marketing campaigns aim to revitalize demand. The Q3 earnings call included some positive remarks on the Fossil brand’s performance from Nick Jonas’s campaign and higher price point products. Semrush’s web traffic data shows stabilization and even some growth for Fossil from mid-2025 forward, but it remains to be seen whether the shift marks the start of a longer term trend.

Renegotiated minimum license royalties and core market prioritization are other key operational changes. Fossil is moving towards a third-party distribution model in its smaller markets, aiming to cut operational costs. Fossil has aggressively cut down on its global direct-to-consumer store network and aims to continue store closures to right-size the business; Fossil has closed 50 stores during the past year, having a total of 204 remaining stores at the end of Q3.

Lower promotions and a focus on higher-margin wholesale partners aim to improve margins. Right-sizing is ultimately expected to cut revenue further to just $800 million by 2027, but to improve Fossil’s margins and to position the company for better long-term growth. Fossil aims to achieve a mid-to-high single-digit adjusted operating margin by 2027.

The changes have clearly been positive so far. While Fossil continues to lose revenues as a result of the turnaround, profitability has already improved clearly. The Q3 report showed a -$14.9 million adjusted operating loss, improving by $7.1 million year-on-year due to significant SG&A cuts. Similar margin progress should continue over the short- to mid-term as Fossil’s channel refocus and other initiatives continue. Fossil’s brand stabilization could bode well if brand indicators continue to show progress.

I believe that the turnaround is still on volatile grounds – Fossil is operating in a very difficult long-term market environment. At some point, Fossil needs to stabilize its revenue base, and visibility for such stabilization is low while Fossil continues to right-size the business. A decade-long decline weighs odds against such stabilization; Fossil, Michael Kors, and Empirio Armani still need to gain a much better brand foothold. Progress on improving profitability is clearly positive, but long-term expectations shouldn’t be set too high.

FOSL Stock Is a Balanced Gamble

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Fossil to estimate a fair value for the stock. I assume revenues to decline to $800 million for 2027, and to continue on a weak path until 2030 where revenues stabilize at $700 million. The assumption factors in Fossil’s turnaround right-sizing, but also a slow overall brand outlook.

I expect the adjusted EBIT margin to stabilize at 5% from 2027 forward, within Fossil’s target range. The cash flow conversion should stand good after taxes as Fossil has minimal CapEx. Restructuring weighs somewhat on the cash flow outlook, which Fossil will likely require in upcoming years to address declining sales.

The estimates put Fossil’s fair value at $3.8, quite near the stock price as of writing. I believe that the stock’s current valuation makes the investment a balanced risk-to-reward gamble – better-than-expected earnings stabilization could create further upside, but a failed turnaround could deteriorate shareholder value. High debt makes the investment volatile.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.04%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I assume an 8.5% long-term interest rate, in line with Fossil’s refinanced notes’ interest. I assume quite a high 50% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.23% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. Seeking Alpha estimates Fossil’s beta at 1.35. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity stands at 10.37% and the WACC at 9.04%.

Takeaway

Fossil’s weak long-term performance is being addressed as the company is on a multi-year turnaround path. Right-sizing the business towards highest-margin businesses has made good progress in stabilizing profitability, but concerning long-term market trends, uncertain brand positioning, thin margins, and high debt make the outlook volatile regardless. As FOSL stock’s valuation seems volatile but balanced at the current level, I initiate the stock at a Hold rating.

data.title: Meta Just Proved The AI Bulls Right
data.description:
Introduction

On January 8, I rated Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) a Strong Buy based on the following thesis:

For me, META is a Buy thanks to its

1. Growth levers of user expansion, broadening monetization & ad placement

2. AI increasing targeting of ads, resulting in pricing and engagement increases

3. Low valuation offers a solid margin of safety.

The stock has done well since, but it is too early to perform a meaningful performance assessment. More importantly, Meta reported their Q4 and thereby FY2025 earnings today in after-market trading, giving us an opportunity to test and potentially change or expand this thesis. The stock is up 8.5% in response, which is already a good sign.

Financials

Meta reported Q4 revenue of $59.9B, up roughly 24% YoY, driven by continued strength in advertising demand across its Family of Apps. Revenue came in clearly ahead of consensus expectations of about $58.3–58.4B, representing a beat of a little over $1.5B. Operating income was $24.7B, implying an operating margin of 41%.

Diluted EPS was $8.88, increasing around 11% YoY. This also exceeded analyst estimates, which were in the low $8.20 range. In my view, these results highlight Meta’s ability to sustain strong top-line growth and profit expansion at scale, even as the company ramps up AI and infrastructure investment.

Organic growth helps lead the way, as Meta’s Family of Apps (Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, WhatsApp) continues to scale, with family daily active people around 3.58 billion, up mid-single digits year-over-year. Ad impressions and average price per ad both increased, supporting revenue growth. A big part of this came from Reels, as mentioned in the earnings call:

Reels had another strong quarter with Watchtime up more than 30% Y/Y in the U.S. Engagement is benefiting from several optimizations we made to improve the quality of recommendations, including simplifying our ranking architecture to enable more efficient model scaling. - CFO Susan Li.

Operating and free cash flows grew 29% and 6.5% YoY, respectively, showing continued strong cash generation. This helps them to continue to invest without having to issue too much debt and buy back some shares at the same time. I like this financial structure much more in the AI trade than some others that solely rely on debt and dilution to fund their future revenue sources, which is much more risky.

Regarding 2026, the company issued the following statement (emphasis added):

We expect first quarter 2026 total revenue to be in the range of $53.5-56.5 billion. Our guidance assumes foreign currency is an approximately 4% tailwind to year-over-year total revenue growth, based on current exchange rates.

We expect full year 2026 total expenses to be in the range of $162-169 billion.

  • The majority of expense growth will be driven by infrastructure costs, which includes third-party cloud spend, higher depreciation, and higher infrastructure operating expenses.
  • The second-largest contributor to total expense growth is employee compensation, driven by investments in technical talent. This includes 2026 hires to support our priority areas, particularly AI, as well as a full year of expenses from 2025 hires.
  • At a segment level, we expect expense growth to be driven by the Family of Apps, with Reality Labs operating losses remaining similar to 2025 levels.

We anticipate 2026 capital expenditures, including principal payments on finance leases, to be in the range of $115-135 billion, with year-over-year growth driven by increased investment to support our Meta Superintelligence Labs efforts and core business.

Despite the meaningful step up in infrastructure investment, in 2026 we expect to deliver operating income that is above 2025 operating income.

Absent any changes to our tax landscape, we expect our full year 2026 tax rate to be 13-16%.

They did not provide any concrete profitability metrics, which makes it harder to forecast future earnings. Operating profit is said to be higher than in 2025, but we do not know by how much. Still, this is a sign of incredible operating leverage when, despite being in the biggest investment cycle Meta has ever been in, skyrocketing operating expenses, an increase in profits can be recorded. What is then going to happen when expenses normalize and the company focuses more on profitability?

AI beneficiary?

In the most direct sense, Meta could be making revenues and profits from selling AI services, as Zuckerberg hinted:

In 2025, we rebuilt the foundations of our AI program. Over the coming months, we're going to start shipping our new models and products. I expect our first models will be good, but more importantly, we'll show the rapid trajectory that we're on. And then I expect us to steadily push the frontier over the course of the year as we continue to release new models. - CEO Mark Zuckerberg.

At the same time, Reality Labs still weighs on profits ($6B loss in Q4 2025), but there is light at the end of the tunnel, as operating losses are expected to inflect soon:

I expect Reality Labs losses this year to be similar to last year, and this will likely be the peak as we start to gradually reduce our losses going forward while continuing to execute on our vision as we plan. - CEO Mark Zuckerberg.

AI is also going to indirectly benefit Meta, by increasing ad pricing, which I outlined in my previous coverage, but also by significantly boosting employee efficiency. If the whole organization of tens of thousands of employees gets much more done, the business can grow so much faster. With AI still developing and improving, efficiency gains should also become larger from here.

Since the beginning of 2025, we've seen a 30% increase in output per engineer, with the majority of that growth coming from the adoption of AI-generated coding, which saw a big jump in Q4. We're seeing even stronger gains with power users of AI coding tools whose output has increased 80% year over year. We expect this growth to accelerate through the next half. - CFO Susan Li.

Valuation

At a forward P/E ratio of just 23, META's organic growth levers, high margins, capital efficiency, and operating leverage seem almost fully disregarded. There are also no Reality Labs or direct AI product profits priced into the stock, which now seem more likely to manifest sometime in the future. Even if they continue at a loss, the company is undervalued. After another strong report today, it's likely that analysts will revise their EPS targets upwards, making the "real" forward P/E ratio even smaller. The same is true for their P/OCF ratio, a metric to gauge how much cash they are able to produce once CapEx normalizes, is at 13.4, which is very low as well. Both of these imply upside potential just from a multiples perspective.

Risks

As noted above, losses from Reality Labs and AI spending might continue to drag down META's profits longer than expected and specifically at a lower ROI than expected. Zuckerberg is not particularly known for a solid track record of great capital allocation. The worst case of that, however, would likely be a small underperformance, and once these segments are divested or shut down, the stock can perform well again.

Another aspect is that despite the growing number of daily active people, saturation of users could be reached in a few quarters or years, as there are only so many billion people on this earth. What directly ties into that are proposed bans of social media and restrictions globally, specifically for teens. The fact that the CFO addresses this in the earnings report shows it is a significant business risk for Meta.

Finally, we recently aligned with the European Commission on further changes to our Less Personalized Ads offering, which we will begin rolling out this quarter. However, we continue to monitor legal and regulatory headwinds in the EU and the U.S. that could significantly impact our business and financial results. For example, we continue to see scrutiny on youth-related issues and have a number of trials scheduled for this year in the U.S., which may ultimately result in a material loss. - CFO Susan Li.

Conclusion

The Meta long thesis remains largely the same, as they proved again what the business is capable of, though some upside optionality is added from AI. Meta remains a great investment for the long run at these prices, even after the post-earnings spike.

Rating: Maintain Strong Buy.

data.title: Apple Earnings Preview: Investors Eye Record Revenue And AI Updates Amidst Market Jitters
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Apple Inc. (AAPL) prepares to report its fiscal first quarter 2026 results on Thursday, January 29, after market close. Wall Street will be watching with intrigue, no doubt, as despite a sluggish start to the year for the stock, which is down roughly 6-9% year-to-date, expectations are high.

Analysts project the tech giant will deliver its largest year-over-year revenue jump in four years, driven by a robust holiday season, the iPhone 17 cycle, and double-digit growth in its Services division.

What to Expect?

Wall Street consensus points to a record-breaking quarter for Apple.

  • Revenue: Analysts expect revenue to land around $138.5 billion, representing a growth rate of approximately 10-12% year-over-year. This would easily eclipse the previous record of $124.3 billion set in Q1 2025.
  • Earnings Per Share (EPS): Consensus estimates for EPS are pegged at $2.67, up roughly 11% from $2.40 in the year-ago quarter.

Gross Margin: Investors will look for margins to remain steady between 47-48%, though any impact from rising memory costs or tariffs will be scrutinized.

Key Areas to Focus On - Segment Performance Analysis

While the headline numbers are critical, the narrative during the conference call, specifically regarding future demand and AI, will likely dictate the stock's immediate reaction.

  • iPhone 17 Demand and the Supercycle: The iPhone remains the engine of Apple’s profitability, and Q1 covers the critical holiday sales period. Analysts forecast iPhone revenue to surge by over 12% to approximately $78-$80 billion.

The "supercycle" thesis relies heavily on the iPhone 17 significantly outperforming its predecessors. Early data suggests strong demand for the Pro models, and investors will want confirmation that the upgrade cycle is accelerating, particularly among the estimated 300+ million users with iPhones older than four years.

  • The China Rebound: China has been a battleground for Apple, facing stiff competition from domestic rivals like Huawei and a mixed economic backdrop. However, recent data offers a glimmer of hope: Counterpoint Research indicates Apple may have led the Chinese smartphone market in Q4 with over 20% market share.

A confirmed rebound in Greater China revenue would be a major bullish signal, alleviating fears that Apple is losing its grip on its most important international market.

  • Services Growth: The Services segment (App Store, iCloud, Apple TV+, etc.) is Apple’s profit growth engine. Wall Street is looking for revenue to hit roughly $30 billion, implying ~14% growth.

Sustained double-digit growth here is vital to justify Apple’s premium valuation (approx. 30x forward earnings). Any deceleration could spook investors who view Services as the company's safety net against hardware cyclicality.

  • AI Strategy and "Apple Intelligence": Artificial Intelligence remains the "wild card." While Apple has been quieter than its peers, the integration of "Apple Intelligence" and the partnership with Google (Gemini) to power Siri are central to the long-term thesis.

Market participants will listen intently for updates on consumer adoption of AI features and, more importantly, how Apple plans to monetize them. Commentary on future capital expenditures related to AI servers will also be key.

The path forward is not without significant obstacles. The global memory shortage, the ongoing DOJ antitrust trial, and the complexities of the European regulatory environment present real risks to the company's operating model. The "trillion-dollar tightrope" involves balancing these external pressures while continuing to deliver the "quality and resilience of earnings" that investors have come to expect.

If Apple can demonstrate that it has successfully harnessed the power of generative AI to drive both hardware upgrades and high-margin recurring fees, its premium valuation may be well-justified for years to come.

The more seasoned market participants are watching three main things: whether the sales recovery in China will last, how many people are buying the new AI-powered Pro phones, and the company's plan to make Siri the main controller of a system that is so connected, users will find it very hard to leave.

Potential Implications for the Stock Price

Apple stock is currently trading near a technical support level, testing its October 2025 lows. The market sentiment has been cautiously optimistic, but valuation concerns persist.

  • Bullish Scenario: A "beat and raise" (beating estimates and raising guidance) combined with positive commentary on China and AI could spark a reversal, pushing the stock back toward the $270-$288 range. Analysts at JP Morgan recently raised their target to $315, citing confidence in the multi-year product cycle.
  • Bearish Scenario: If iPhone revenue misses the high expectations or if guidance for the March quarter is tepid due to rising component costs (specifically memory), the stock could break below crucial support levels near $206-$210.

Apple Daily Chart, January 28, 2025

The Bottom Line

This earnings report is about more than just a holiday quarter beat, it is a litmus test for the iPhone 17's longevity and Apple's ability to maintain growth in the AI era. With the stock currently underperforming the broader tech sector in 2026, a strong report is needed to reignite investor momentum.

Original Post

data.title: Meta: Shock And Awe (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

After delivering a triple play for Q4 2025, Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) stock is rising by ~9% to $729 per share in the post-market session. In this report, we break down Meta's latest quarterly results and reassess its long-term risk/reward to reach an informed investment decision.

Dissecting Meta's Q4 2025 Report

For Q4 2025, Meta reported revenues of $59.89B [+24% y/y] and non-GAAP EPS of $8.88 per share, beating consensus estimates of $58.47B and $8.18 per share, respectively.

And, these stronger-than-expected results were driven by continued improvement across Meta's core KPI metrics, with Family of Apps [FOA] DAPs up 7% y/y to 3.58B, FOA Ad Impressions up 18% y/y, and FOA Average Price Per Ad up +6% y/y in Q4 2025.

Now, while Meta exceeded management's CAPEX guide for 2025 of $70-72B by a sliver, total expenses fell within management's guided range of $116-118B. Consequently, Meta delivered operating income of ~$25B, operating cash flow of ~$36B, and free cash flow of ~$14B for Q4 2025.

According to Mark Zuckerberg, Meta founder and CEO,

We had strong business performance in 2025. I'm looking forward to advancing personal superintelligence for people around the world in 2026.

Looking forward, Meta's leadership is guiding for Q1 2026 revenue to come in the range of $53.5-56.5B, well above the pre-ER consensus of $51.4B, thereby completing the triple play for Q4.

Interestingly, Meta's total expense guide of $162-169B calls for a 40% y/y jump in expenses in 2026, with CAPEX expected to rise to $115-135B (from ~$72B in 2025) to support Meta's ongoing quest for superintelligence.

Despite management projecting 2026 operating income to be higher than 2025, I think such heavy CAPEX spending will raise several question marks around ROI in the investor community, given that expense growth is far outpacing revenue growth.

Now, that said, the social media/digital advertising giant has a cash-rich balance sheet and an incredibly cash-generative core business. Furthermore, in order to keep investors on board, Zuckerberg & Co. appear to be taking a step back on Reality Labs (eliminating cash burn growth and ~10% of jobs) and pausing stock buybacks, which fell to nil in Q4 2025. The dividend was held steady in Q4, at $1.34B [$0.525 per share].

Is META Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

In my previous note on the company, I upgraded META stock to a "Buy" after a post-Q3 earnings dip to the mid-to-high $600s:

In our previous update in July, I downgraded Meta to a Hold rating, citing suboptimal long-term risk/reward. However, since then, we have seen a solid time (Meta made decent business progress in Q3) and price correction (Meta stock has declined from $775 per share to $685 per share). Consequently, Meta's 5-year expected CAGR return has improved from ~12% to ~16%, and now exceeds TQI's investment hurdle rate of 15%.

From a valuation standpoint, Meta is once again a Buy.

Now, from a technical perspective, Meta's post-ER selloff is damaging near term; however, the medium-to-long-term setup is still quite bullish, with Meta potentially in the middle of a bullish Wave-5 move as per Elliot Wave Theory, with a target range of $850-1075 per share.

Considering Meta's healthy business performance, reasonable valuation, and constructive technical setup, I am upgrading Meta back to a Buy rating in light of its Q3 2025 earnings dip.

Key Takeaway: I rate META stock a Buy in the high-$600s.

Source: Meta: Buy The Q3 Earnings Dip (Rating Upgrade)

And, with Meta stock jumping up ~9% to upwards of $729 in today's after-hours session, I think my previous buy call was a prescient one.

Now, sticking to our reasonable, long-term growth and margin assumptions, Meta's updated fair value is ~$736.5 per share [or ~$1.89T market cap]. With Meta stock trading right around this level, I believe META is now fairly valued.

Now, predicting where a stock will trade in the short term is impossible; however, over the long run, a stock will track its business fundamentals and obey the immutable laws of money. If the interest rates were to return to artificially low levels (i.e., ZIRP), higher equity multiples would be justifiable. However, I work with the assumption that interest rates will eventually track the long-term average of ~5%. Inverting this number, we get a trading multiple of ~20x (P/FCF).

Assuming a base-case P/FCF (exit) multiple of ~20x, I see Meta stock rising from $729 per share to $1438 per share at a CAGR of ~14.5% over the next five years.

As you may know, TQI's investment hurdle rate is 15%. With Meta failing to exceed this level on the back of its post-Q4 surge, it is no longer a buy under our valuation process. The patient long-term investor who is willing to accept slightly lower returns can still allocate fresh capital here, but as a rules-based investor, I am going to have to wait for a better entry point or a time correction to increase my allocation to META.

With Meta's infrastructure spending set to outpace its revenue expansion rates, I can see Mr. Market affording us another bite at the cherry in the next 6-12 months. Also, from a technical perspective, Meta appears to be in a 5-wave EWT move to the downside right now, which points to a culmination in the ~$425-575 range.

Considering the balance of business fundamentals, valuation, and technicals, I am downgrading META stock back to a "Hold" rating.

Key Takeaway: I rate META stock a Hold in the mid-$700s.

Thanks for reading, and happy investing. Please share your thoughts, concerns, and/or questions in the comments section below.
